stories.headline: Artemis II Crew Preps for Final Days Before Historic Moon Launch
stories.hook: With launch day just around the corner, NASA's four-person crew is running final checks before becoming the first humans to leave Earth orbit in over 50 years.
stories.body:
NASA's Artemis II crew is in the final stretch of launch preparations at Kennedy Space Center ahead of their April 1 liftoff window. Astronauts Reid Wiseman, Victor Glover, Christina Koch, and Canadian Space Agency astronaut Jeremy Hansen completed emergency egress rehearsals and suited up for pre-launch countdown simulations this week, with mission managers declaring the Orion spacecraft and Space Launch System rocket in excellent health.

The crew will fly a free-return trajectory around the Moon — a path first used during Apollo 13 — that brings Orion within roughly 7,400 kilometers of the lunar surface before slinging back toward Earth. The 10-day mission is designed to validate Orion's life support, navigation, and thermal systems with a live crew aboard. If all goes well, Artemis III — the actual lunar landing — follows within the next two years.

Weather forecasts show acceptable conditions for the April 1 primary window, with backup opportunities through April 6. Public excitement has been building steadily: NASA's live stream of crew activities drew over 12 million concurrent viewers at peak on Friday. For mission controllers who have spent years preparing for this moment, the countdown feels both long overdue and suddenly very real.

image.alt: A rocket launching into the sky with bright exhaust flames and billowing smoke

links.title: NASA: Artemis II Mission Page
Space.com: Artemis 2 launch countdown updates
Reuters: NASA crew ready for Moon mission launch

stories.headline: Greenland Is Sitting on a Vast Platinum Deposit — and That Changes Everything
stories.hook: A new geological survey has identified one of the world's largest untapped platinum group metal deposits beneath Greenland's ice, reigniting debates over Arctic sovereignty and mining.
stories.body:
A joint Danish-Canadian geological survey published this week in Nature Geoscience has identified an enormous deposit of platinum group metals — including platinum, palladium, and rhodium — beneath southwestern Greenland's Julianehåb intrusive complex. The deposit is estimated to contain tens of millions of ounces, potentially ranking among the largest of its kind ever discovered. Platinum group metals are essential for hydrogen fuel cells, catalytic converters, and a growing range of clean energy technologies.

The timing is geopolitically loaded. Greenland's semi-autonomous government, Naalakkersuisut, has long pursued greater economic independence from Denmark, and resource revenues are central to that strategy. The survey has prompted renewed interest from mining companies in Canada, Australia, and the United States, while simultaneously intensifying discussions about who controls what beneath the melting ice — a question that has grown thornier as climate change exposes previously inaccessible terrain.

Environmentalists are raising alarms about the impact of large-scale mining on Greenland's pristine ecosystems and indigenous Inuit communities who depend on them. The Greenlandic government has insisted it will set the terms for any development and is under no pressure to rush. But with the clean energy transition driving insatiable demand for these metals, pressure from outside will be hard to resist.

image.alt: Vast icy landscape in Greenland with blue glacial waters and snow-covered mountains
image.credit: Photo by Visit Greenland on Unsplash

links.title: Nature Geoscience: Julianehåb platinum group metal deposit
BBC: Greenland's huge platinum discovery
Financial Times: Greenland minerals race heats up

stories.headline: Scientists Find Life Thriving in the Deepest, Darkest Trenches of the Ocean
stories.hook: A deep-sea expedition has discovered hundreds of previously unknown species living in total darkness miles below the surface — some using completely alien metabolic strategies.
stories.body:
An international deep-sea research expedition using next-generation submersibles has returned from the Atacama Trench with an extraordinary haul of biological data. The team documented over 300 previously uncatalogued species in depths between 6,000 and 8,000 meters, including novel forms of polychaete worms, crustaceans, and microbial mats that appear to derive energy through chemosynthesis from sulfur compounds seeping from hydrothermal vents.

One of the most striking finds was a translucent fish that appeared to use bioluminescence in a pattern never before observed — rapid sequential flashing along its lateral line — which researchers speculate may serve as a form of communication in the otherwise pitch-black environment. The team also recovered sediment cores containing microbial communities that genomic analysis suggests have been isolated from surface life for more than 50,000 years.

The discoveries underscore how little humans understand of deep-ocean ecosystems, which cover more than half of Earth's surface. The findings carry implications for astrobiology: if life can thrive in such extreme conditions here, the prospects for life in the ice-covered oceans of Jupiter's moon Europa or Saturn's Enceladus improve considerably. The research team's full dataset will be made publicly available through the Ocean Biodiversity Information System.

image.alt: Ethereal deep-sea ocean creatures glowing in the dark depths of the ocean
image.credit: Photo by Noé Sardet on Unsplash

links.title: Nature: New species from the Atacama Trench expedition
National Geographic: Hundreds of new deep-sea species discovered
The Guardian: Life in the abyss — what the deepest ocean reveals

stories.headline: Cows Can Use Symbolic Tools to Solve Problems, Study Finds
stories.hook: A new cognitive study reveals that cattle can learn to use simple tools to obtain rewards — a finding that reshapes how scientists think about farm animal intelligence.
stories.body:
Researchers at the University of Edinburgh's Royal (Dick) School of Veterinary Studies have published findings suggesting that domestic cattle possess more sophisticated problem-solving abilities than previously recognized. In a series of controlled trials, cows were trained to associate symbolic markers — colored panels — with food reward locations, and then taught to use a simple lever mechanism to access a treat when other methods were blocked. Twelve of sixteen animals successfully transferred the learned behavior to novel contexts, suggesting genuine cognitive flexibility rather than rote conditioning.

The researchers also observed that cattle who solved the puzzle faster showed elevated heart rates and vocalized more, which the team interpreted as signs of excitement or motivation. This emotional engagement with a cognitive task mirrors findings from studies on pigs and horses but has rarely been documented in cattle with this level of rigor.

The implications extend beyond academic curiosity. If cattle can engage meaningfully with problem-solving tasks, farming practices that deny cognitive stimulation may constitute a welfare concern not previously accounted for in industry standards. Several European agricultural bodies are already reviewing welfare guidelines in light of growing evidence of farm animal cognition. The researchers say their next study will examine whether cattle can learn from watching other individuals — a capacity known as social learning.

image.alt: Cows grazing in a green field on a sunny farm day
image.credit: Photo by Harper Smith on Unsplash

links.title: Applied Animal Behaviour Science: Cognitive flexibility in domestic cattle
BBC Science: Cows are smarter than we thought
New Scientist: Cattle can use tools to solve problems

stories.headline: Long COVID's Grip on the Immune System Is Finally Coming Into Focus
stories.hook: A landmark study has identified a specific pattern of immune dysregulation in long COVID patients — and suggests a potential therapeutic target.
stories.body:
A multi-institution research consortium has published the most comprehensive immunological analysis of long COVID to date, tracking immune cell populations in 1,400 patients over 18 months. The results, published in Cell, reveal a consistent pattern: persistently elevated levels of interferon-gamma and a population of dysfunctional CD8+ T cells that remain activated but fail to clear viral remnants. The pattern is most pronounced in patients with the most debilitating symptoms, including fatigue, cognitive fog, and exercise intolerance.

Critically, the researchers found that patients whose symptoms improved over time showed a measurable normalization of this immune signature, while those with persistent symptoms did not. This suggests the immune dysregulation is not a byproduct of illness but a potential driver of ongoing symptoms. The findings validate what many long COVID patients have argued for years: that their condition reflects genuine biological dysfunction, not psychosomatic illness.

The most actionable finding is a potential therapeutic target. A subset of patients showed significant symptom improvement after treatment with Baricitinib, a JAK inhibitor already approved for rheumatoid arthritis, which dampens the over-active interferon response. A randomized controlled trial is now planned at 12 sites across the US and UK. Experts caution that no single treatment will work for all long COVID patients, given the heterogeneity of the condition, but the study represents a major step toward precision medicine for this population.

image.alt: Microscopic view of immune cells representing viral research and immunology

links.title: Cell: Immune dysregulation in long COVID (landmark study)
STAT News: Long COVID immune study breakthrough
The Atlantic: We finally understand what long COVID does to the immune system

stories.headline: Ancient Woolly Rhino DNA Offers a Blueprint for Arctic Rewilding
stories.hook: Researchers have assembled the most complete woolly rhino genome ever sequenced, and some scientists think it could guide efforts to restore cold-adapted megafauna to the Arctic.
stories.body:
A team at the Centre for Palaeogenetics in Stockholm has sequenced a near-complete woolly rhino genome from a specimen preserved in Siberian permafrost, providing unprecedented detail about the genetic architecture of a species that went extinct roughly 10,000 years ago. The genome reveals specific gene variants associated with cold adaptation — including in genes controlling fat deposition, thermoregulation, and hair follicle density — that distinguish the woolly rhino from its surviving African relatives.

The research, published in Current Biology, is primarily an exercise in evolutionary genomics, but it has attracted attention from a niche but growing community of de-extinction researchers. The Colossal Biosciences team, which has been engineering cold-adapted traits into the Asian elephant genome as a first step toward resurrecting the woolly mammoth, says the rhino genome could inform parallel efforts for a woolly rhino analog using the Sumatran rhino — already critically endangered — as a base genome.

Conservation biologists are split. Skeptics argue that resources devoted to speculative de-extinction projects would be better spent protecting the species we still have. Proponents counter that woolly megafauna played an irreplaceable role in maintaining Arctic grassland ecosystems — through grazing, trampling, and nutrient cycling — and that restoring them could help stabilize permafrost and slow methane release. The genome itself is a scientific milestone regardless of how that debate resolves.

image.alt: A rhinoceros standing in a natural grassland habitat
image.credit: Photo by Daley van de Sande on Unsplash

links.title: Current Biology: Complete woolly rhino genome
Science: Woolly rhino genome decoded from permafrost specimen
New York Times: Could we bring back the woolly rhino?

stories.headline: Flowers Are Losing Their Scent — and Pollinators Are Paying the Price
stories.hook: Air pollution is chemically degrading floral scent trails, disrupting the signals that bees and other pollinators rely on to find food.
stories.body:
A new study from UC Riverside and the Max Planck Institute for Chemical Ecology has quantified something that farmers and beekeepers have long suspected: air pollution is chemically degrading the volatile organic compounds that give flowers their scent, disrupting the olfactory signals that pollinators use to locate food sources. The study found that in areas with high ozone and nitrogen dioxide levels, the scent plume from flowering plants was degraded by up to 90% within a few hundred meters — far shorter than the distances at which bees typically detect and navigate toward flowers under clean-air conditions.

The researchers used controlled chamber experiments and field measurements near highways and agricultural areas to track the breakdown of specific floral terpenes and esters. They found that pollinators in polluted areas spent significantly more time searching and covered less ground per foraging trip, reducing the efficiency of pollination. For crops that depend on insects for fertilization — including almonds, blueberries, and many vegetables — this represents a measurable agricultural risk that has been largely overlooked.

The findings have immediate policy implications. Current air quality standards are set primarily based on human health outcomes; the study argues that protecting pollinator navigation should be incorporated as a criterion. Some European environmental agencies have already signaled openness to reviewing standards. In the meantime, the researchers suggest that planting flower corridors in areas with lower pollution exposure could help sustain pollinator populations.

image.alt: A bee collecting nectar from a vibrant yellow flower in bloom
image.credit: Photo by Aaron Fernando on Unsplash

links.title: Science: Air pollution degrades floral scent signals
The Guardian: Flowers are losing their scent due to air pollution
Nature Food: Pollinator navigation disruption and crop yields

stories.headline: Ozempic May Protect the Brain — New Data Links GLP-1 Drugs to Lower Dementia Risk
stories.hook: People taking semaglutide for diabetes or weight loss appear to have significantly lower rates of Alzheimer's disease and other dementias, according to a large observational study.
stories.body:
A retrospective analysis of health records from over 1.2 million patients in the US and UK has found that people prescribed GLP-1 receptor agonists — including semaglutide (Ozempic/Wegovy) and liraglutide — had a 40 to 70% lower incidence of new Alzheimer's diagnoses over a five-year period compared to matched controls on other diabetes medications. The effect was strongest in patients who had been on the drugs for more than two years and was present even after adjusting for weight loss, suggesting the benefit may go beyond metabolic improvements.

The biological mechanism isn't fully understood, but researchers point to several hypotheses. GLP-1 receptors are expressed in the brain, particularly in regions associated with memory and cognition. Animal studies have shown that GLP-1 agonists can reduce neuroinflammation, clear amyloid plaques, and protect dopaminergic neurons. Earlier studies had shown benefits in Parkinson's disease models; this is the first large-scale human data specifically linking the drug class to reduced Alzheimer's incidence.

Experts caution that observational studies can't prove causation, and randomized controlled trials are now underway at multiple sites. Novo Nordisk, Eli Lilly, and several academic research centers have launched or accelerated trials of GLP-1 drugs specifically for Alzheimer's prevention. If confirmed, the implications would be enormous: Alzheimer's affects over 50 million people globally, and there are currently no disease-modifying treatments available for most patients.

image.alt: Brain scan imagery representing neuroscience and cognitive health research
image.credit: Photo by Gaspar Uhas on Unsplash

links.title: NEJM: GLP-1 agonists and dementia risk — observational study
STAT News: Ozempic and Alzheimer's risk — the new data
NPR: Weight loss drugs may protect against dementia, study suggests

stories.headline: A Nearby Star Is Behaving in Ways Physics Can't Fully Explain
stories.hook: Gamma Cassiopeiae, a well-studied star just 550 light-years away, is emitting X-rays at levels that defy the standard models for hot, massive stars.
stories.body:
Astronomers using the XMM-Newton space telescope have published a new analysis of Gamma Cassiopeiae, a B-type emission star located about 550 light-years from Earth, that deepens a longstanding mystery. The star emits X-rays at levels roughly 10 times higher than other stars of similar type, but the X-rays have an unusual thermal profile — hot, but not as hot as expected from standard models of wind-shock interactions — and they are variable in ways that don't correlate with the star's rotation period.

Several competing hypotheses have been proposed over the decades. One suggests that Gamma Cas has a neutron star or white dwarf companion in a close orbit, and that mass transfer between them generates the excess X-rays. Another argues that the star's rapid rotation creates a disk of ejected material that interacts with the magnetic field to produce shock-heated plasma. A third, more speculative idea involves a magnetic dynamo driven by subsurface convection in a type of star not usually thought to have one.

The new XMM-Newton data rules out some variants of the companion star hypothesis but cannot definitively distinguish between the remaining explanations. What makes this case scientifically important beyond the star itself is the discovery of at least 23 other nearby stars with similar X-ray signatures — suggesting Gamma Cas may be the prototype of a new class of stellar objects rather than an isolated anomaly. Understanding them could reshape models of how massive stars lose mass and evolve.

image.alt: A stunning nebula with stars glowing in deep space representing astronomy and the cosmos
image.credit: Photo by Scott Lord on Unsplash

links.title: Astronomy & Astrophysics: New XMM-Newton analysis of Gamma Cassiopeiae
Sky & Telescope: Gamma Cas — the star that defies explanation
ESA: XMM-Newton reveals new clues about enigmatic Gamma Cas

stories.headline: Archaeologists Uncover a Lost Bronze Age City in Iraq's Marshlands
stories.hook: Drone mapping and targeted excavation have revealed a previously unknown Bronze Age settlement in southern Iraq's marshes — intact for 4,000 years under layers of sediment.
stories.body:
A joint Iraqi-Italian archaeological team has announced the discovery of a substantial Bronze Age settlement in the Mesopotamian Marshlands of southern Iraq, preserved beneath meters of alluvial sediment deposited over millennia. The site, located near the town of Chibayish in Dhi Qar province, covers approximately 30 hectares and appears to date from roughly 2000 to 1600 BCE. Initial excavations have uncovered monumental mudbrick architecture, administrative tablets written in cuneiform, and extensive evidence of bronze working.

The site's preservation is remarkable. The waterlogged, anaerobic conditions of the marshland environment — the same that has allowed the Iraqi marshes to shelter unique ecosystems for thousands of years — protected organic materials that would normally have decomposed. The team recovered wooden artifacts, woven textiles, and botanical remains including cereal grains and date palm seeds that will allow detailed reconstruction of the local agricultural economy.

The discovery is particularly significant for understanding third-millennium Mesopotamian trade networks. Several of the cuneiform tablets appear to be administrative records referencing commodity exchanges with cities further north along the Tigris-Euphrates system. If analysis confirms this, the site may represent a previously unrecorded commercial hub in the southern Mesopotamian trading network — one whose existence was erased by the same floods that paradoxically preserved it.

image.alt: Ancient ruins and archaeological remains from an ancient civilization excavation
image.credit: Photo by Mr. Great Heart on Unsplash

links.title: Antiquity: Bronze Age settlement discovered in the Iraqi Marshlands
BBC History: Lost Bronze Age city found in Iraq's ancient marshes
Smithsonian Magazine: Iraqi marshland reveals a lost Mesopotamian city

stories.headline: AI Agents Are Learning to Use Computers the Way Humans Do
stories.hook: A new wave of 'agentic' AI systems can now browse the web, fill forms, write and execute code, and navigate software interfaces with increasing competence — and companies are racing to deploy them.
stories.body:
A cluster of new research papers and product announcements this week highlight the rapid maturation of agentic AI systems — programs that don't just generate text but actually take actions in digital environments. Anthropic's Claude, OpenAI's Operator, and Google's Project Mariner have all reached milestones on standard web-navigation benchmarks in the past month, with the leading systems now completing complex multi-step browser tasks — filling out forms, purchasing items, booking travel — at success rates above 80% on standardized evaluations.

The commercial deployments are beginning. Several Fortune 500 companies have quietly rolled out AI agents for internal workflows: reviewing supplier contracts, processing insurance claims, and handling IT ticket resolution. Early reports suggest meaningful productivity gains in narrow, well-defined task domains, though failure rates in novel situations remain higher than what most enterprises would accept for customer-facing applications.

The safety challenges are becoming more concrete as capabilities advance. Researchers have demonstrated "prompt injection" attacks in which malicious content embedded in a webpage or document hijacks an agent's behavior — a problem that becomes more serious as agents gain access to more powerful tools and sensitive systems. The question of how much autonomy AI systems should have, and under what oversight structures, is shifting from philosophical to operational at a pace that has caught many organizations off guard.

image.alt: Futuristic AI robot and technology representing artificial intelligence automation
image.credit: Photo by Luan Fonseca on Unsplash

links.title: MIT Technology Review: The race to build AI agents
The Verge: AI agents are taking over digital tasks — here's what that means
ArXiv: Benchmarking agentic systems on web navigation tasks

stories.headline: The Puffiest Planet Ever Found Is Getting Stranger Under Scrutiny
stories.hook: Kepler-51d is a planet roughly the mass of Saturn but nearly the volume of Jupiter, and new observations are revealing an atmosphere unlike anything astronomers have modeled.
stories.body:
New spectroscopic observations of Kepler-51d, a so-called 'super-puff' exoplanet orbiting a young star about 2,600 light-years away, have revealed an atmosphere containing an unexpected abundance of photochemical hazes at high altitudes — essentially the same kind of smoggy haze that gives Titan its orange color, but on a planetary scale and in a very different stellar environment. The observations, made using JWST's NIRSpec instrument, also found evidence for rapid atmospheric escape, with hydrogen and helium streaming away from the planet at unusually high rates.

Kepler-51d has a density similar to cotton candy — about 0.1 grams per cubic centimeter — making it one of the least dense planets ever discovered. The new data suggests its extreme puffiness is not just a consequence of orbital heating but may be maintained by an active chemical cycle in which photochemical reactions continuously regenerate haze particles that expand the apparent radius of the atmosphere.

The planet is a useful laboratory for understanding atmospheric physics at extremes. Its youth — the host star is only about 500 million years old, less than one-tenth the age of our Sun — means it offers a window into a phase of planetary evolution that most known exoplanets have long since passed through. Understanding what determines whether a young, volatile-rich planet retains or loses its atmosphere has direct implications for modeling the early history of Earth and the solar system's other rocky planets.

image.alt: An exoplanet and its star in deep space representing planetary science and astronomy
image.credit: Photo by NASA Hubble Space Telescope on Unsplash

links.title: Nature Astronomy: JWST spectroscopy of Kepler-51d
NASA: JWST reveals cotton-candy planet's weird atmosphere
Science News: Super-puff planet Kepler-51d is even stranger than expected

stories.headline: Your Phone's Constant Notifications Are Literally Reshaping Your Brain
stories.hook: New neuroimaging data shows that heavy smartphone notification use is associated with measurable changes in prefrontal cortex activity linked to attention regulation.
stories.body:
A three-year longitudinal neuroimaging study conducted at University College London has found that young adults who received high volumes of smartphone notifications showed measurable changes in the structure and function of prefrontal cortex regions associated with sustained attention, impulse control, and cognitive flexibility. Participants who averaged more than 80 notifications per day showed thinner gray matter in the right dorsolateral prefrontal cortex compared to low-notification users — a difference that remained significant after controlling for smartphone use in general.

The functional imaging data was equally striking. When asked to sustain attention on a single task, high-notification users showed greater activation in the anterior cingulate cortex — a region associated with conflict monitoring — as if their brains were working harder to suppress the urge to check for new information. They also performed significantly worse on tasks requiring continuous attention for periods longer than four minutes.

The researchers are careful to note that the causal arrow could run in multiple directions: people with pre-existing differences in attention regulation may be more likely to enable large volumes of notifications. But the longitudinal design — tracking the same individuals over three years — does show that within-person changes in notification volume correlate with within-person changes in the neural measures, strengthening the case for a causal effect. The study adds to a growing body of evidence that the design choices embedded in smartphone software have real consequences for cognitive development.

image.alt: A smartphone screen with multiple notification alerts representing digital distraction

links.title: Nature Human Behaviour: Smartphone notifications and prefrontal cortex
The Guardian: Smartphone notifications are reshaping the brain, study finds
Wired: Your notifications are changing your brain

stories.headline: AI Can Now Generate Fake X-Rays Convincing Enough to Fool Radiologists
stories.hook: Researchers have demonstrated that AI-generated synthetic medical images can pass detection by trained radiologists and automated screening systems — raising urgent questions about diagnostic integrity.
stories.body:
A study published in Radiology has demonstrated that AI-generated synthetic chest X-rays and CT scans can fool experienced radiologists at rates well above chance. The research team used a diffusion model trained on a large proprietary dataset to generate synthetic images that either appeared normal or were seeded with realistic-looking pathologies — including nodules, infiltrates, and signs consistent with early-stage lung cancer. Radiologists who were not told the images might be synthetic misclassified synthetic images as real approximately 69% of the time, and standard AI-based screening software performed no better.

The implications are dual-edged. On the beneficial side, high-quality synthetic medical images could help address the scarcity of labeled training data for diagnostic AI systems and be used to train radiologists on rare conditions. Several medical imaging companies have already deployed synthetic data pipelines for exactly this purpose.

On the malicious side, the researchers demonstrate that synthetic medical images could be used to fabricate diagnostic evidence — whether to defraud insurance systems, manipulate disability determinations, or create false documentation in legal proceedings. The paper calls for the development of authentication standards for medical images, analogous to digital watermarking approaches being explored for photographs and video. The FDA has acknowledged the issue and says a working group is examining whether existing medical device regulations cover AI-generated diagnostic content.

image.alt: A medical X-ray displayed on a lightboard representing radiology and diagnostic imaging

links.title: Radiology: AI-generated synthetic X-rays fool radiologists
MIT Technology Review: AI deepfakes are coming for medical imaging
STAT News: Fake medical images and the authentication gap

stories.headline: Antarctica's Hektoria Glacier Is Collapsing Faster Than Any Model Predicted
stories.hook: New satellite data shows Antarctica's Hektoria glacier retreating at double the rate previously projected, raising the specter of significant sea level rise within decades rather than centuries.
stories.body:
Satellite altimetry and synthetic aperture radar data analyzed by a team at the British Antarctic Survey and NASA's Jet Propulsion Laboratory shows that the Hektoria glacier on the Antarctic Peninsula has retreated at an average rate of 9.2 kilometers per year over the past three years — more than double the projections of the most pessimistic climate models. The acceleration began following a series of marine heat waves that destabilized the glacier's floating ice tongue, leaving the grounded ice exposed to warmer ocean water at its base.

Hektoria is not among the largest glaciers in Antarctica, but its behavior is being watched closely as a bellwether for the broader Antarctic Peninsula. The region has warmed faster than almost any other place on Earth over the past 50 years, and several glaciers that were considered stable a decade ago are now showing signs of rapid dynamic change. The concern is not just the ice that Hektoria itself contains — enough to raise global sea levels by about 8 centimeters — but whether its collapse could destabilize neighboring glaciers through a process called marine ice cliff instability.

The findings arrive as negotiators prepare for a crucial round of climate finance discussions under the UNFCCC framework. Sea level rise projections have been a contentious point in those talks, with low-lying island nations and coastal megacities pushing for more aggressive near-term action. Observations like those from Hektoria are systematically shifting the scientific consensus toward the higher end of previously published sea level rise scenarios.

image.alt: A vast glacier with ice calving into the ocean representing Antarctic ice loss and climate change
image.credit: Photo by Matt Palmer on Unsplash

links.title: Nature Climate Change: Accelerated retreat of Hektoria Glacier
NASA JPL: New data on Hektoria Glacier collapse
The Guardian: Antarctica's Hektoria glacier melting twice as fast as feared
